title: I watched a video on YouTube. The title was "Kalshi 15 min markets, arbitrage, bonding on Polymarket (guy bets 7mil to make 0,2%)"
author: Fine-Perspective-438
selftext:
https://reddit.com/link/1sgnxsv/video/buw6c9svn5ug1/player

Someone explains how they bet $7M on near-certain outcomes on Polymarket for 0.2% returns. The video also covers arbitrage between Kalshi's 15-minute crypto markets and Polymarket.



"While watching, I thought. Can't this just be automated?"



So I gave the video URL to an AI and asked. "Can you create an investment strategy after watching this video?"



The AI analyzed the video and summarized the key points.

1) Arbitrage opportunities between Kalshi and Polymarket.

2) A bonding strategy. betting large on resolved or near-certain markets for small repeated gains.

3) Black swan risk warning.



Then I asked a follow-up. 

"If we combine the Kalshi plugin and the Kraken plugin, can we automate this strategy?"



The AI said 

"yes."



= Use the Kalshi plugin to fetch 15-minute market prices and place YES/NO orders.

= Use the Kraken plugin to fetch real-time spot prices and place hedge orders.

= Every 15 minutes, compare prices from both sides. If arbitrage exists, execute on both.



Copying someone's strategy was this easy.

★This was just to see if copying a YouTube strategy is even possible. not to argue whether it actually works.

Good investing might just be good copying.

comments.author: whemstreet
comments.body: Lol let me know how that works out for you

comments.author: Dependent_Stay_6954
comments.body:
This is a cool experiment, but there’s a big gap between “automatable” and “actually profitable”.
What you’ve described is basically:
cross-venue arbitrage (Kalshi ↔ Polymarket)
a bonding / near-certainty carry trade
automated execution loop
That sounds clean, but in practice there are some hard constraints people underestimate:
1. The arb probably doesn’t exist in a scalable way
If there’s a true price mismatch:
it gets closed very fast
or it’s size-limited
Prediction markets especially are:
thin liquidity
wide spreads
slow fills
So even if you detect arb every 15 mins, you likely can’t size it meaningfully.
2. Execution risk kills this before logic does
The “just execute both sides” assumption is doing a lot of work.
Real issues:
one leg fills, the other doesn’t
latency between APIs
partial fills
price moves during execution
That turns “risk-free” into directional exposure instantly.
3. The 0.2% edge is not free money
That kind of return profile only works if:
you can recycle capital continuously
AND avoid a single large loss
But as you already noted:
black swan risk
That’s the entire game here. One bad event wipes months of small gains.
4. The bonding strategy is basically picking up pennies in front of a truck
Near-certain bets aren’t actually risk-free:
oracle risk (resolution errors)
platform risk
timing risk (market resolves later than expected)
People underestimate how often “99%” events fail in these markets.
5. Automation is the easy part — edge is the hard part
Yes, this can be automated. That’s trivial.
But:
if a strategy is easy to automate and widely known, it’s usually already arbitraged away
6. The real takeaway (this part is valuable)
What you’ve done here is actually the right workflow:
extract strategy logic
translate to execution steps
identify automation path
That’s exactly how real systems are built.
TLDR:
Yes, this is automatable.
No, that doesn’t mean it’s profitable.
The bottleneck isn’t code — it’s:
liquidity
execution
and whether the edge actually survives costs + real-world friction.
If you really want to test it properly, the next step is:
simulate fills (not just prices)
include latency + partial fills
cap position size by order book depth
That’s where 90% of these strategies die.

replies.author: Fine-Perspective-438
replies.body:
Thank you for the detailed analysis. These are truly important points.

I think we agree. This post was about "Can AI understand and replicate YouTube strategies?", not "Is this strategy profitable?". 

The point you raised in number 6 is the core issue: 

logic extraction → conversion to execution phase → identification of the automation path. This aligns exactly with the experiments I conducted.

The issues you mentioned regarding liquidity, execution risk, and partial execution are real problems. This is precisely the gap between "automatability" and "profitability," and it is an area that requires in-depth analysis.

If you would like to try using broker plugins (such as Kalshi or Kraken) yourself, please refer to the following link: [https://github.com/kokogo100/sandclaw](https://github.com/kokogo100/sandclaw)

It is 100% free and open source, and works even with zero balance in your broker account.
